case_title: MARLAYNA ALYSSA JACKSON VS. FCA US, LLC. ET AL

docket_entries.description: CIVIL CASE COVERSHEET FILED (TRANSACTION ID # 74656871) FILED BY PLAINTIFF JACKSON, MARLAYNA ALYSSA
NOTICE TO PLAINTIFF

LAW & MOTION, DEPT. 301, AS TO THE FEB-03-2025 HEARING RE: DEFENDANT FCA US, LLC.'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT; THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. THE DEMURRER IS CONTINUED FOR HEARING TO MARCH 3, 2025 (SEE MINI MINUTES FOR COMPLETE RULING). COUNSEL FOR THE PREVAILING PARTY IS TO PREPARE AND SUBMIT A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH REPEATS VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE COURT'S RULING WITH A COVER LETTER OUTLINING COMPLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE: CHRISTINE VAN AKEN; CLERK: V. DA FONSECA; NOT REPORTED. (301/CVA)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Dismissal Under Code Of Civil Procedure Section 473(B) And Reinstate Case; Declaration Of Madelyn O?Kelley-Bangsberg In Support Thereof
TENTATIVE RULING: On the Law and Motion / Discovery calendar for June 18, 2026, line 4. PLAINTIFF MARLAYNA JACKSON'S Motion To Set Aside Dismissal Under Code Of Civil Procedure Section 473(B) And Reinstate Case.

Transferred to be heard in department 206 before Judge East on July 7, 2026, at 9:30 a.m. Movant seeks to vacate Judge East's dismissal.

For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number.

Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing.

The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Feb-02-2026 Dismissal

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Apr-09-2025 Set For Trial On Feb-02-26 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Feb-18-2025 Continued To Apr-23-25 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-03-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT FCA US, LLC. DEMURRER to COMPLAINT

Defendant FCA US, LLC's demurrer to the sixth cause of action in Plaintiff's complaint, a claim for fraudulent inducement to contract by concealment, is overruled.

The elements of this claim are "(1) misrepresentation (false representation, concealment, or nondisclosure); (2) knowledge of falsity (scienter); (3) intent to defraud (i.e., to induce reliance); (4) justifiable reliance; and (5) resulting damage." (Dhital v. Nissan North America, Inc. (2022) 84 Cal.App.5th 828, 845 (Dhital). FCA correctly argues that fraud must be pleaded with particularity. FCA contends that the sixth cause of action is insufficiently particular because it does not allege the "how, when, where, to whom, and by what means" the allegedly fraudulent representations were tendered. (Stansfield v. Starkey (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 59, 74 [italics omitted].)

But FCA does not address Dhital, a case considering the adequacy of similar pleadings. In Dhital, plaintiffs brought a Song-Beverly Act claim against a car manufacturer. Along with Song-Beverly claims, they alleged "that Nissan, by intentionally concealing facts about the defective transmission, fraudulently induced them to purchase a car." (Dhital, 84 Cal.App.5th at p. 838.) The court of appeal held that plaintiffs adequately pleaded their fraudulent inducement claim by pleading that "the CVT transmissions installed in numerous Nissan vehicles (including the one plaintiffs purchased) were defective; Nissan knew of the defects and the hazards they posed; Nissan had exclusive knowledge of the defects but intentionally concealed and failed to disclose that information; Nissan intended to deceive plaintiffs by concealing known transmission problems; plaintiffs would not have purchased the car if they had known of the defects; and plaintiffs suffered damages in the form of money paid to purchase the car." (Id. at p. 844.) Plaintiff's fraudulent inducement claims contain similar allegations at similar levels of detail. (Compl. paras. 65-72; para. 18 ["Plaintiff purchased the Subject Vehicle as manufactured with FCA's defective engine."].) Dhital applies the established rule that when fraud occurs by omission or concealment, a plaintiff is not required to describe with particularity every conversation with a defendant's agent when the disclosure could or should have happened. (See Alfaro v. Community Housing Improvement System & Planning Assn., Inc. (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 1356, 1384.) FCA's reliance in its reply brief on Committee on Children's Television, Inc. v. General Foods Corp. (1983) 35 Cal.3d 197, is unavailing; in that case the Supreme Court excused the plaintiffs from describing each sugary-cereal advertisement alleged to be fraudulent with particularity, in light of the volume of advertisements and the fact that the defendants were better positioned to know what ads aired and when. (Id. at p. 217.)

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)

DEFENDANT'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT (ADDED FOR POSTING OF PART 2 OF 2 OF THE TENTATIVE RULING)
Off Calendar Mar-03-2025 Off Calendar

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-03-2025 Continued To Mar-03-25 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 301
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT FCA US, LLC.'s DEMURRER to COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff failed to respond to defendant's meet and confer efforts. Plaintiff filed its opposition late and submitted a CCP 473(b) declaration. The court will accept the late-filed opposition. The court orders plaintiff to meet and confer concerning the issues raised in defendant's demurrer within five court days of the date of entry of this order. Failure to meet and confer pursuant to this order may subject plaintiff to sanctions. Defendant may file a written reply brief within 10 court days of entry of this order unless the matter is mooted by plaintiff's filing of an amended complaint. The demurrer is continued for hearing to March 3, 2025.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling with a cover letter outlining compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)
